name: Holiday
description: Stories in which a seasonal occasion functions as both structural deadline and emotional catalyst, forcing characters to confront estrangement, regret, or longing within a compressed window of time. The holiday is never mere backdrop â€” its traditions, rituals, and communal expectations create the pressure that makes transformation not just possible but necessary. (This graph has 18 nodes — one more than the 17-node standard — because it includes a second Anti-Pattern node to capture an additional genre-specific pitfall.)

nodes.label: Holiday Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story in which a seasonal occasion serves simultaneously as deadline, mirror, and catalyst â€” the approaching holiday makes avoidance impossible, reflects what has been lost or broken, and creates the conditions in which emotional transformation, reconnection, or redemption can finally occur. Holiday stories do not simply happen during a holiday; the holiday is the engine of the entire narrative, and the promise is that something frozen will thaw before the season ends.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as a Holiday narrative
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that anchor the holiday as the story's structural and emotional spine
typical_variants: Estranged family reuniting by Christmas morning
Romance rekindled under Valentine's deadline
Cynical character transformed by the spirit of the season
Grief interrupted and eventually softened by holiday ritual
failure_modes: Holiday is only setting dressing with no bearing on the conflict or resolution
Transformation occurs for reasons unconnected to the holiday's specific pressures or meanings
signals_in_text: Opening establishes the approaching holiday as a felt presence
Characters' emotional states are explicitly tied to the seasonal occasion
Holiday traditions and rituals are introduced early as load-bearing story elements

nodes.label: Holiday as Structural Deadline
nodes.definition: The holiday must function as an immovable narrative deadline â€” a date or moment that the story is inexorably moving toward, creating urgency and preventing characters from indefinitely deferring change. Unlike ordinary story deadlines, the holiday deadline is culturally loaded: missing it or failing to meet its emotional demands carries communal as well as personal cost. The ticking clock is not arbitrary but tied to the specific meaning of the season.
exit_conditions: The holiday's arrival (or failure to arrive meaningfully) marks the story's climax or resolution point
typical_variants: Christmas Eve as the final deadline for reconciliation
New Year's midnight as the moment of transformation or loss
Valentine's Day as the day a confession must be made or forever withheld
Thanksgiving dinner as the gathering that cannot be escaped
failure_modes: Holiday arrives but the story's resolution is disconnected from it
Deadline is felt by the narrator but never by the characters
Story could be resolved identically in any other month
signals_in_text: Characters count down to the holiday explicitly or implicitly
Pacing accelerates as the holiday approaches
Key decisions are framed in terms of what is possible before the holiday versus after

nodes.label: Reconnection or Renewal as Central Drive
nodes.definition: Holiday stories are fundamentally about repairing or restoring something that has been broken, severed, or allowed to atrophy â€” a relationship, a sense of belonging, a person's capacity for joy or hope. The holiday tradition of gathering and togetherness makes estrangement acutely visible and reunion emotionally available in ways that ordinary time does not. Without a genuine rupture to be healed and a genuine drive toward its healing, the story lacks its essential motor.
exit_conditions: At least one broken bond, lapsed connection, or lost capacity for joy is identified early and drives the plot toward repair
typical_variants: Estranged family members forced together by holiday obligation
Romantic partners separated by circumstance or misunderstanding
Individual who has lost belief in the season's meaning rediscovering it
Community or tradition at risk of disappearing, fought for by protagonist
failure_modes: Story involves a holiday but no meaningful estrangement or loss to be healed
Reconnection happens without dramatic obstacle or cost
The rupture is cosmetic and the resolution requires no real change
signals_in_text: Early scenes establish what has been lost or broken
Characters visibly resist or long for connection
Holiday traditions serve as the medium through which reconnection is attempted

nodes.label: Emotional Transformation Must Occur
nodes.definition: The holiday must produce a genuine internal change in at least one character â€” not a change of circumstance but a change of heart, perspective, or willingness. Holiday stories carry the structural promise of before and after: the character who arrives at the story's opening is measurably different from the character who inhabits the story's close. This transformation must be earned through resistance, cost, and the specific pressures the holiday applies, not simply declared.
exit_conditions: Character's internal state at the story's close is demonstrably different from the opening, and the difference is traceable to the holiday's events
typical_variants: Cynic who rediscovers generosity or wonder
Grieving character who reaches acceptance
Estranged character who chooses forgiveness
Self-isolated character who chooses connection
failure_modes: Character is the same person at the end as the beginning
Transformation is announced but not dramatized
Change results from a revelation rather than from sustained pressure and choice
signals_in_text: Opening scenes clearly establish the character's emotional wound or defensive posture
Middle scenes show the holiday applying increasing pressure on that posture
Closing scenes show the character acting in ways that would have been impossible at the story's opening

nodes.label: Seasonal Setting Is Essential, Not Incidental
nodes.definition: The specific season and its associated holiday must be organically necessary to the story â€” the cold and dark of winter intensifying the warmth of Christmas reunion, the fertility and harvest abundance of autumn deepening a Thanksgiving story's themes of gratitude and loss. The story must be impossible to transplant to a neutral time of year without losing its meaning. Seasonal imagery, weather, light, and associated cultural rituals must do thematic work, not merely atmospheric decoration.
exit_conditions: Seasonal details appear throughout the story as carriers of thematic meaning, not just set dressing
typical_variants: Winter's darkness and cold as metaphor for emotional isolation that warmth can thaw
Autumn's harvest and decay as metaphor for gratitude and mortality
Spring's renewal as metaphor for second chances on Valentine's or Easter stories
Halloween's confrontation with death and the uncanny externalizing hidden fears
failure_modes: Season could be removed or swapped without affecting the story's meaning
Seasonal details are mentioned but carry no thematic resonance
Story's emotional arc is entirely independent of the season's cultural associations
signals_in_text: Weather, light, and seasonal imagery are integrated into emotional scenes
Characters' internal states are described through seasonal metaphors
Seasonal traditions and their cultural meanings are explicitly or implicitly active in the plot

nodes.label: Christmas / Winter Holiday
nodes.definition: The dominant Holiday subgenre, in which Christmas or the broader winter holiday season provides the deadline and the symbolic framework of warmth against cold, light against darkness, and generosity overcoming selfishness. The Christmas story has its own deep genre conventions â€” the transformation of a miser or cynic, the reunion of sundered family, the miracle that breaks through rational resistance â€” and readers arrive with strong expectations about the emotional arc. Stories in this subgenre must either honor those conventions with freshness or deliberately subvert them with awareness.
entry_conditions: The holiday in question is Christmas, Hanukkah, Winter Solstice, or the broader winter holiday season
exit_conditions: Story resolves by or during the holiday, delivering the emotional warmth the season promises
typical_variants: Scrooge arc (cynical transformation)
Family reunion drama
Holiday romance
Holiday mystery or supernatural visitation
Christmas miracle story
failure_modes: Story uses Christmas trappings but delivers cynicism or nihilism as its actual message without meaningful transformation
Sentimentality replaces earned emotion â€” surface warmth without earned cost
signals_in_text: Decorations, carols, gift-giving, and seasonal gatherings appear as load-bearing story elements
Cold and darkness outside contrast with warmth and light inside, mirroring the character's inner journey
Christmas morning or Christmas Eve functions as the climactic moment

nodes.label: Halloween / Spooky Holiday
nodes.definition: Holiday stories set around Halloween or similar death-adjacent holidays (Day of the Dead, All Souls' Night) in which the thinning of the veil between ordinary life and the uncanny creates the conditions for confronting what has been buried, denied, or feared. Unlike pure Horror, the Halloween Holiday story uses spooky elements to externalize emotional truths â€” the ghost that returns is a metaphor for grief unprocessed, the monster is the fear that must be named to be defeated. The holiday's cultural permission to confront darkness is itself the catalyst for transformation.
entry_conditions: The holiday is Halloween, Day of the Dead, or a culturally equivalent death/fear-adjacent seasonal occasion
exit_conditions: Characters confront what they have been hiding or avoiding, facilitated by the holiday's specific permission to engage with the uncanny and the deceased
typical_variants: Ghost story where the haunting is unresolved grief
Coming-of-age story about confronting childhood fear
Romantic comedy with spooky complications
Family story centered on honoring deceased ancestors
failure_modes: Halloween is merely aesthetic â€” costumes and candy with no engagement with the holiday's deeper themes of mortality and the return of the repressed
Spooky elements displace rather than amplify the emotional transformation
signals_in_text: Death, memory of the dead, and confronting fears are central themes
Halloween rituals carry thematic weight beyond atmosphere
Transformation involves naming or facing something that was denied or buried

nodes.label: Valentine's Day / Romance Holiday
nodes.definition: Holiday stories in which Valentine's Day (or an equivalent romance-coded seasonal occasion) creates a deadline for love to be declared, repaired, or released. The cultural pressure of Valentine's Day makes romantic longing acutely visible â€” it is impossible to feel neutral about love on this day â€” and this heightened emotional visibility is the engine of the subgenre. Stories must use the holiday's romantic expectations actively, either as the pressure that brings lovers together or as the painful mirror that reveals a love that has failed.
entry_conditions: The holiday in question is Valentine's Day or another culturally romance-coded seasonal occasion
exit_conditions: The romantic question at the story's heart is resolved â€” declaration made, reunion achieved, or loss acknowledged â€” by or on the holiday
typical_variants: Will-they-won't-they resolved by Valentine's declaration
Couple in crisis reconnecting before the holiday reveals how far they've drifted
Lonely character finding unexpected connection on the most couple-centric day of the year
Former lovers crossing paths again
failure_modes: Romance is resolved for reasons having nothing to do with the holiday's pressure
Valentine's Day is only set dressing and could be replaced by any other deadline
Romantic resolution is unearned â€” characters get together without confronting the obstacle that separated them
signals_in_text: Valentine's Day expectations and pressures are explicitly active in characters' minds
Romantic tension is directly tied to what the holiday demands or reveals
Declaration or reunion occurs at the holiday's climactic moment

nodes.label: Thanksgiving / Harvest / Gratitude Holiday
nodes.definition: Holiday stories centered on Thanksgiving or equivalent harvest and gratitude occasions, in which the enforced gathering of family around a shared table serves as a crucible for unresolved resentments, competed loyalties, and the difficult work of genuine thankfulness. The Thanksgiving story is structurally unique because its holiday ritual â€” the gathering at a table from which no one can gracefully leave â€” makes avoidance impossible in a way that other holidays do not. Gratitude, in these stories, is never easy or automatic; it must be fought for against grief, bitterness, or regret.
entry_conditions: The holiday is Thanksgiving or a culturally equivalent occasion centered on harvest, gathering, and explicit gratitude
exit_conditions: The enforced gathering produces either genuine reconciliation or a definitive rupture that clears the way for a new beginning
typical_variants: Dysfunctional family dinner that becomes the site of long-deferred confrontation
Return of the prodigal family member
Gratitude story about recognizing what one nearly lost
Multi-generational family reckoning with change and continuity
failure_modes: Thanksgiving dinner is a set piece without dramatic function â€” the gathering produces no confrontation, revelation, or change
Family tensions are raised but tidily resolved without real cost or change
Gratitude is the story's theme but no character earns it through suffering or resistance
signals_in_text: The family table and the meal preparation are load-bearing narrative elements
Characters cannot escape each other once the gathering begins
Dialogue around the table carries the weight of everything unsaid all year

nodes.label: Holiday Trappings Are Load-Bearing
nodes.definition: In Holiday stories, the material elements of the season â€” decorations, traditional foods, gift-giving rituals, seasonal music, specific garments and ceremonies â€” must carry thematic and emotional weight, not merely atmosphere. A broken ornament must mean something about the broken family. The refusal to put up the tree must express a character's grief or resistance. The gift that is the wrong thing must embody a misunderstanding between people. Holiday objects are the story's emotional vocabulary, and every significant holiday element introduced must do work.
exit_conditions: At least three significant holiday elements appear in scenes where they carry meaning beyond decoration
typical_variants: Heirloom object carrying family history and grief
Tradition disrupted or restored as the emotional climax
Gift as the physical form of a character's love, misjudgment, or sacrifice
Seasonal food or music as a trigger for memory and longing
failure_modes: Holiday decorations and traditions appear extensively but none of them connect to the story's emotional content
Objects that should be symbolically resonant are treated as pure set dressing
Traditions are described but no character cares about them
signals_in_text: Narrative attention dwells on specific holiday objects with emotional charge
Characters' reactions to holiday elements reveal their internal states
The presence or absence of traditional elements marks the story's emotional arc

nodes.label: Community or Family Gathering Is Required
nodes.definition: Holiday stories require at least one gathering scene in which multiple characters who carry unresolved feelings toward each other are brought into sustained proximity by the holiday's social demand. The gathering is the holiday genre's equivalent of the confined setting in drama â€” it eliminates avoidance and forces interaction. Without the gathering, the holiday's power to compress and accelerate emotional dynamics cannot operate. The gathering need not be a single meal or party; it can be a sustained period of enforced togetherness (a holiday visit, a multi-day family stay).
exit_conditions: At least one scene gathers the story's key characters in a shared space from which they cannot gracefully withdraw
typical_variants: Christmas dinner with the whole estranged family
Holiday party where protagonist must face someone they've been avoiding
Thanksgiving gathering that becomes the site of long-deferred confrontation
Halloween neighborhood event or costume party
failure_modes: Characters remain isolated throughout a holiday story, undermining the genre's communal premise
Gathering occurs but characters do not interact in ways that advance the emotional plot
Gathering is resolved too easily â€” characters retreat before the real confrontation can occur
signals_in_text: Scene with multiple characters in shared space where exit would be socially costly
Conversations at the gathering carry double meanings or suppressed history
Physical proximity forces revelations or confrontations that would not occur in separate spaces

nodes.label: Ticking Clock of the Holiday Deadline
nodes.definition: The holiday's specific date creates a felt urgency that accelerates pacing and forecloses delay â€” characters know that the window for transformation is finite and closing. Unlike a generic deadline, the holiday deadline carries cultural weight: what is not said or done before Christmas morning carries a different quality of loss than what is deferred to some ordinary Tuesday. The ticking clock must be felt in the story's pacing and in the characters' awareness, not merely noted in passing.
entry_conditions: Holiday deadline established in the narrative
exit_conditions: Pacing noticeably accelerates as the holiday approaches, and characters' decisions are framed in terms of what is possible before the holiday versus after
typical_variants: Countdown to Christmas Eve when the family departs
Valentine's Day morning as the last possible moment for a declaration
Thanksgiving dinner start time as the hard deadline for an arrival or decision
New Year's midnight as the absolute limit for transformation
failure_modes: Holiday deadline is mentioned but has no effect on pacing or urgency
Characters behave as if they have unlimited time despite the approaching holiday
Story's resolution occurs well before the holiday's climactic moment, draining the deadline of meaning
signals_in_text: Explicit or implicit countdown to the holiday's key moment
Characters aware they must act now or miss the window
Scene transitions that compress time as the holiday approaches

nodes.label: Broken Tradition or Disrupted Celebration Scene
nodes.definition: Every Holiday story must contain a scene in which a tradition is broken, a celebration is disrupted, or the holiday fails to deliver the warmth and connection it promises â€” this is the genre's dark night of the soul, the moment when the gap between what the holiday is supposed to mean and what it actually delivers is at its widest. This scene externalizes the story's central wound and makes it undeniable. Without this rupture, the eventual restoration of celebration has no emotional cost and therefore no emotional weight.
entry_conditions: Central emotional wound or estrangement established
exit_conditions: A specific tradition is visibly broken or celebration is visibly disrupted in a scene that makes the stakes undeniable
typical_variants: The family dinner that collapses into argument or silence
The gift-giving moment that reveals a fundamental misunderstanding
The tradition that cannot be performed because of loss, absence, or change
The celebration that the protagonist cannot bring themselves to participate in
failure_modes: Story never shows the failure of the holiday's promise â€” moves directly from setup to restoration without the disruption
Disruption occurs but is treated as minor rather than as the story's emotional nadir
Broken tradition is resolved too quickly before its full weight has been felt
signals_in_text: Scene has a palpable quality of wrongness â€” the holiday failing to feel like a holiday
Characters' faces and silences carry the weight of what the scene represents
The broken element (the empty chair, the failed meal, the undecorated tree) becomes a symbol the story returns to

nodes.label: Moment of Reconnection or Forgiveness
nodes.definition: The Holiday story's climactic emotional beat is the scene in which the central estrangement is bridged â€” where a character chooses forgiveness over resentment, reaches across the distance that has separated them, or allows themselves to be reached. This scene is the genre's payoff and must be earned through all that preceded it: the established rupture, the pressure of the gathering and the deadline, the disrupted tradition that made the cost of continued estrangement undeniable. The reconnection must feel chosen and costly, not automatic.
entry_conditions: Characters have been brought into proximity by gathering
Holiday deadline pressure is near its peak
exit_conditions: At least one character makes a definitive choice to forgive, reach out, or accept connection that was previously refused or unavailable
typical_variants: The apology that was never given, finally spoken
The gesture that crosses the distance (the embrace, the returned call, the unopened gift finally opened)
The confession that lets another person in
The decision to stay rather than leave
failure_modes: Reconnection occurs without any clear choice or cost â€” characters simply stop being estranged
The moment is interrupted before it lands
Reconnection is told rather than shown â€” summarized in narration rather than dramatized
Character reconnects but has not actually changed â€” the underlying wound is unchanged
signals_in_text: Scene is slower and more intimate than the scenes surrounding it
Physical gesture carries the weight of the verbal content
Both characters visibly changed by the exchange
Holiday imagery present in the scene amplifies the emotional content

nodes.label: Celebration-Restored Scene
nodes.definition: Holiday stories must close with a scene in which the holiday's promise is finally and genuinely delivered â€” not as it was before (since genuine transformation has occurred), but in a new form that incorporates what has been lost and what has been gained. This scene is not mere happy ending sentimentality; it demonstrates that the characters' transformation is real by showing them inhabiting the holiday differently than they could at the story's opening. The celebration is restored, but the people celebrating it are changed.
exit_conditions: Final scene shows characters participating in the holiday's rituals with a quality of presence and meaning that was impossible at the opening
typical_variants: Christmas morning with the family reunited, the empty chair acknowledged but not only mourned
The holiday meal completed with new understanding between those at the table
The tradition renewed in a form that honors what has changed
The celebration attended by the protagonist who could not attend at the opening
failure_modes: Final scene simply restores the status quo without acknowledging the transformation
Celebration restored but feels identical to how it would have looked before the story â€” no change is visible
Story ends at the reconnection moment without showing the characters in the restored holiday
signals_in_text: Holiday elements from the opening reappear, but with altered meaning
Characters' emotional presence in the celebration contrasts visibly with their earlier state
Tone shifts to earned warmth â€” not easy sentimentality but hard-won joy

nodes.label: Warm, Nostalgic, Sentimental Tone with Earned Emotion
nodes.definition: Holiday stories require a tone of warmth and nostalgia, but the genre's central tonal challenge is keeping that warmth earned rather than manufactured. Sentimentality that has not been paid for with genuine cost and resistance is the genre's characteristic failure â€” it feels false and manipulative. The correct tone is one in which warmth is hard-won: characters have genuinely suffered, genuinely resisted, and genuinely chosen before the story is allowed to be warm. Nostalgia is the right register because the season is itself saturated with memory, but nostalgia for Holiday stories should always carry the bittersweet knowledge of what time and change have taken.
exit_conditions: Warmth in the closing scenes is preceded by genuine resistance, cost, and choice earlier in the story
typical_variants: Austere warmth â€” spare, quiet, emotionally reserved until the earned moment
Abundant warmth â€” lush seasonal imagery, generous emotion, but grounded in genuine dramatic cost
Bittersweet warmth â€” joy shadowed by acknowledged loss
failure_modes: Warmth is present from the opening with no dramatic cost â€” the story feels like a greeting card rather than a story
Nostalgia tips into saccharine â€” the past is idealized without acknowledgment of its complications
Seasonal sentiment is deployed in lieu of character work
signals_in_text: Warm scenes follow and therefore earn their warmth from preceding cold or hard scenes
Nostalgic imagery carries both pleasure and pain
Characters are allowed to feel joy only after they have paid for it in honest emotion

nodes.label: Cynicism Punished Rather Than Transformed
nodes.definition: Holiday stories fail when a cynical character is defeated, humiliated, or externally forced into celebrating rather than genuinely transformed from within. The genre's promise is transformation â€” a change of heart that the character chooses, however reluctantly. When the story instead punishes the cynic through embarrassment, circumstantial coercion, or social pressure without internal change, it delivers the form of transformation while violating its substance. The reader may feel the correct emotional notes in the moment but will leave the story feeling something was false, because nothing real changed in the character.
entry_conditions: Story features a cynical or resistant character
exit_conditions: Character's change is internal and chosen, traceable to genuine emotional shifts rather than external force or humiliation
typical_variants: Cynic embarrassed into participating but never actually believing
Skeptic overwhelmed by magical events that short-circuit rather than earn belief
Resistant character simply worn down by others' enthusiasm rather than moved by genuine encounter
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Climactic transformation scene relies on external event or pressure rather than internal choice
Character's final warm behavior could be explained by compliance rather than genuine change
Story cannot identify what specifically changed in the character's interior

nodes.label: Holiday as Mere Backdrop
nodes.definition: Holiday stories fail entirely when the holiday functions only as setting atmosphere â€” providing seasonal imagery, music, and costumes but bearing no causal or thematic relationship to the plot's conflict or resolution. This anti-pattern is the genre's most fundamental violation because it mistakes decoration for structure. If the story's central conflict could unfold identically in July, the holiday has not done its job. The holiday must be the reason the characters are together, the source of the deadline, the mirror that reflects the wound, and the medium through which healing occurs.
entry_conditions: Story is set during a holiday
exit_conditions: Holiday is causally necessary to at least three of the following: the characters' gathering, the deadline, the conflict's nature, the resolution's method
typical_variants: Romance that happens to occur at Christmas but has no Christmas-specific dynamic
Family drama that uses Thanksgiving as set dressing for a conflict that has nothing to do with gathering or gratitude
Halloween story where the spooky setting is purely aesthetic with no engagement with the holiday's deeper themes
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Holiday references can be removed from scenes without affecting their meaning
Characters do not think or speak about the holiday in emotionally meaningful ways
The story's resolution would occur at the same time and in the same way regardless of the seasonal occasion

edges.label: Promise requires the holiday to function as deadline
preconditions: Holiday Promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Establishes that the story has a hard temporal boundary with cultural and emotional weight
edges.effects_on_character: Characters know time is running out, making deferral increasingly costly
common_alternatives: Some Holiday stories use the holiday's aftermath rather than its approach as the deadline â€” the question of what was left undone
anti_patterns: Holiday arrives with no narrative urgency â€” characters are not racing against it

edges.label: Promise requires a rupture to be healed
preconditions: Holiday Promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Centers the story's emotional stakes in a specific broken or threatened bond
edges.effects_on_character: Characters have something to lose (further estrangement) and something to gain (reunion) that the holiday makes urgent
common_alternatives: Some Holiday stories focus on discovering a new connection rather than restoring a broken one
anti_patterns: Story has no meaningful rupture â€” characters are fine and the holiday merely adds cheer

edges.label: Promise requires genuine internal change
preconditions: Holiday Promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Establishes that the story's payoff is internal change, not merely circumstantial resolution
edges.effects_on_character: The character who arrives at the story's end must be measurably different from the character at its opening
common_alternatives: Ensemble Holiday stories may distribute transformation across multiple characters rather than concentrating it in one protagonist
anti_patterns: Story resolves external conflict but no character changes internally

edges.label: Promise requires season to be load-bearing
preconditions: Holiday Promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: The season's specific meanings â€” its cultural associations, its weather, its rituals â€” must be active in the story's emotional vocabulary
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' inner lives are expressed through seasonal imagery and seasonal rituals
common_alternatives: The specific holiday may shift (some stories are deliberately vague about which winter holiday) but the season must remain essential
anti_patterns: Seasonal details are present but purely decorative â€” the story has no seasonal soul

edges.label: Promise requires earned warmth and nostalgia
preconditions: Holiday Promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone signals that the story will deliver the emotional warmth the holiday promises, but will require the reader to earn it alongside the characters
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' arc toward warmth must be dramatized through resistance and cost, not simply assumed
common_alternatives: Halloween Holiday stories may use a darker or more uncanny variant of this tone, with warmth expressed as comfort in the face of mortality
anti_patterns: Warmth is the story's default register from the opening â€” there is nowhere to go emotionally

edges.label: Winter holiday deadline anchors Christmas subgenre
preconditions: The holiday is Christmas or the broader winter season
edges.effects_on_stakes: Christmas Eve or Christmas morning becomes the absolute deadline for transformation
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are measured against the holiday's specific promise of warmth, generosity, and belonging
common_alternatives: New Year's Eve / New Year's Day as the winter holiday deadline with different thematic connotations (renewal, resolution)
anti_patterns: Christmas is the setting but no specific Christmas deadline creates urgency

edges.label: Halloween reconnection involves confronting the dead or the buried
preconditions: The holiday is Halloween or a death-adjacent seasonal occasion
edges.effects_on_stakes: Reconnection may involve reconnecting with the memory of the deceased, or with a part of the self that was repressed or feared
edges.effects_on_character: The thing being reconnected with may be frightening â€” the holiday gives permission to face it
common_alternatives: Halloween stories may focus on reconnection between the living, with supernatural elements as metaphor
anti_patterns: Halloween story in which the dead and the scary are purely external threats with no connection to the protagonist's interior life

edges.label: Valentine's deadline makes romantic declaration urgent
preconditions: The holiday is Valentine's Day or a romance-coded seasonal occasion
edges.effects_on_stakes: Valentine's Day is the cultural moment when romantic feeling must be expressed or acknowledged â€” silence on this day is itself a statement
edges.effects_on_character: Characters cannot maintain romantic ambiguity through Valentine's Day without that ambiguity becoming a definitive answer
common_alternatives: Some Valentine's stories use the holiday as the mirror that reveals a love has ended rather than as the catalyst for its beginning
anti_patterns: Valentine's Day is the setting but characters are not in any way pressured by the holiday's romantic expectations

edges.label: Thanksgiving gathering as forced reconnection
preconditions: The holiday is Thanksgiving or a harvest/gathering occasion
edges.effects_on_stakes: The enforced gathering makes reconnection unavoidable â€” estranged characters must sit at the same table
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must confront their estrangement in the context of a holiday whose explicit theme is gratitude â€” the gap between what they feel and what the day demands is the story's central tension
common_alternatives: Some Thanksgiving stories focus on the community beyond immediate family â€” neighbors, friends, found family â€” as the gathering
anti_patterns: Characters skip the Thanksgiving gathering and the story does not treat this avoidance as a significant failure or choice

edges.label: Christmas trappings must carry emotional weight
preconditions: Christmas subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every tree, ornament, carol, and gift in the story is a potential carrier of the story's emotional content
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' relationships to Christmas objects reveal their emotional states â€” the character who cannot put up the tree is telling us something
common_alternatives: Stories may focus on one or two specific Christmas objects rather than the full range of holiday trappings
anti_patterns: Christmas decorations are described extensively but none of them connect to any character's emotional experience

edges.label: Halloween trappings carry symbolic weight about death and fear
preconditions: Halloween subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Costumes, decorations, and rituals that engage with death and the uncanny must reflect the story's thematic content
edges.effects_on_character: What a character chooses to dress as, or what they are afraid of, tells us about what they are hiding or avoiding
common_alternatives: Day of the Dead stories use altars, photographs, and food offerings as the load-bearing objects
anti_patterns: Halloween imagery is atmospheric but never connected to character psychology or story theme

edges.label: Valentine's Day creates an absolute romantic deadline
preconditions: Valentine's subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: The specific date creates urgency that makes ambiguity increasingly untenable
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must decide before the day ends or accept that their silence has spoken for them
common_alternatives: Clock may count down to a specific Valentine's event (dinner reservation, party) rather than the day itself
anti_patterns: Valentine's Day passes without the characters acknowledging the deadline it imposed

edges.label: Thanksgiving requires the enforced family gathering
preconditions: Thanksgiving subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: The dinner table becomes the arena â€” avoidance is socially impossible and departure before dessert is a statement
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must endure proximity to those they have been successfully avoiding for months or years
common_alternatives: Friendsgiving or community gathering variants allow the enforced proximity without blood family
anti_patterns: Thanksgiving story where the gathering is optional and several key characters simply don't attend without consequence

edges.label: Load-bearing trappings make their disruption devastating
preconditions: Holiday objects and traditions have been established with emotional weight
edges.effects_on_stakes: When the established tradition breaks, its weight falls on the audience who has been shown what it means
edges.effects_on_character: The disruption externalizes the story's central wound in undeniable, visible form
common_alternatives: Disruption may be the absence of the tradition rather than its active destruction â€” the tree not decorated, the dinner not held
anti_patterns: Tradition is disrupted but the story has not done the work of establishing why it mattered, so the disruption lands without weight

edges.label: Gathering creates the conditions for the celebration's failure
preconditions: Gathering has occurred
edges.effects_on_stakes: Being together makes the failure of togetherness more painful â€” the presence of people who should be close makes their emotional distance undeniable
edges.effects_on_character: Characters cannot escape witnessing (and being witnessed in) the celebration's failure
common_alternatives: Disruption may precede the gathering â€” a character failing to arrive is itself the broken tradition
anti_patterns: Gathering occurs but celebration proceeds smoothly with no disruption or tension

edges.label: Deadline pressure forces the moment of reconnection
preconditions: Holiday deadline is approaching its climactic moment
edges.effects_on_stakes: Time running out makes the reconnection feel urgent and the choice to make it costly â€” it requires doing now what could have been safely deferred
edges.effects_on_character: Character must act before the window closes â€” the deadline strips away the comfort of 'eventually'
common_alternatives: Some Holiday stories reverse this: the deadline passes without reconnection and the aftermath is the story's real climax
anti_patterns: Deadline is near but characters reconnect casually, without any sense that time was a factor

edges.label: Disrupted celebration makes the need for reconnection undeniable
preconditions: Broken tradition scene has established the cost of continued estrangement
edges.effects_on_stakes: The failure of the celebration proves that the status quo is untenable â€” something must change
edges.effects_on_character: Characters have now seen what continued distance costs â€” the choice to reconnect is informed by having witnessed the alternative
common_alternatives: Some stories insert additional scenes between the disruption and the reconnection to allow for resistance and doubt
anti_patterns: Broken tradition occurs but characters immediately reconnect without the disruption having any effect on their choice

edges.label: Earned reconnection allows celebration to be genuinely restored
preconditions: Reconnection moment has occurred with genuine cost and choice
edges.effects_on_stakes: The restored celebration is now carrying the weight of everything the characters went through to get there
edges.effects_on_character: Characters inhabit the restored celebration differently â€” with more presence, more awareness, more earned joy â€” than they could have at the story's opening
common_alternatives: Some Holiday stories end with a muted or partial restoration â€” the celebration is imperfect but more honest than before
anti_patterns: Celebration is restored but it looks identical to what it would have been if nothing had happened â€” the transformation leaves no visible mark

edges.label: Internal transformation enables the choice to reconnect
preconditions: Character's internal change is sufficiently advanced
edges.effects_on_stakes: Without internal transformation, reconnection is a surface gesture â€” true reconnection requires the character to have become capable of it
edges.effects_on_character: The reconnection scene is the external expression of the internal change that has occurred â€” it can only happen because the character has already changed
common_alternatives: In some stories, the reconnection scene is itself the moment of transformation â€” the choice to reach out is the change
anti_patterns: Characters reconnect but neither has changed â€” the same dynamic that produced the estrangement is still intact

edges.label: Transformation must be internal â€” external coercion is not transformation
preconditions: Story features a resistant or cynical character whose change is the narrative goal
edges.effects_on_stakes: If the change is coerced rather than chosen, the story's emotional promise is fraudulent
edges.effects_on_character: The cynic who is beaten into celebration has not transformed; they have been defeated, which is a different and less satisfying story
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern â€” it describes what to avoid

edges.label: Seasonal necessity requires the holiday to be causally active â€” mere atmosphere is not enough
preconditions: Story is set during a holiday but the holiday's causal role in the conflict is unclear
edges.effects_on_stakes: A holiday that is only atmospheric drains the genre's characteristic stakes â€” the urgency, the weight, the specific emotional vocabulary of the season
edges.effects_on_character: Characters in a Holiday story that treats the holiday as backdrop are effectively in a different, lesser story
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern â€” it describes what to avoid
